scope: Same-host Apple Silicon memory profile. This compares Camelid memory-first lazy GGUF Q8_0 serving against MLX-LM public 4-bit weights. It is a resident-memory comparison, not a strict quant-equivalent speed claim.
privacy: Local filesystem paths, hostnames, usernames, and raw cache locations are intentionally omitted.

method.camelid: target/release/camelid serve via scripts/bench-unique-chat.mjs with CAMELID_LAZY_Q8_0_LINEAR=1, CAMELID_RETAIN_Q8_0_BLOCKS=0, CAMELID_FORWARD_RSS_TIMINGS=on, one local server at a time.
method.mlx: scripts/bench-mlx-memory.mjs using mlx-lm in an isolated Python environment; model cache already warm for the committed MLX timing rows.
method.memory_metric: Observed process RSS sampled from ps. Camelid also reports structured forward-pass RSS and Q8 file-read counters.

claim_boundary: Camelid wins the resident-memory comparison in this memory-first profile.
MLX is much faster in this short generation probe; this artifact must not be used as a speed-win claim.
Camelid uses GGUF Q8_0 while the MLX rows use 4-bit MLX weights, so this is directional same-host evidence rather than quant-equivalent benchmarking.
The Camelid memory-first profile trades latency for low RSS by reading Q8_0 weights from file-backed storage during generation.

rows.notes: Model file path intentionally omitted from public artifact.
Model file path intentionally omitted from public artifact.
